theory.intro: The same dataset can be shown as a dot plot, a stem-and-leaf plot, a column graph or a histogram. Each display has a set way of placing every data value onto the picture.
facts: A **dot plot** places one dot for each observation, stacked above its value on a number line.
A **stem-and-leaf plot** splits each value into a **stem** (all but the last digit) and a **leaf** (the last digit), and needs a **key**.
A **column graph** draws an equal-width bar for each category, with gaps between the bars.
A **frequency histogram** draws bars for numerical scores with **no gaps**, each column centred on its score.
steps: Choose the stems from the place value of the data.
Write each leaf next to its stem.
Order the leaves in each row from smallest to largest.
Add a key that shows how to read a stem and a leaf.

foundation.question_text:
A die is rolled $10$ times: $2, 5, 2, 3, 6, 2, 4, 5, 2, 1$.
In a dot plot of this data, how many dots are stacked above the value $2$?

The colours of $10$ counters are recorded: R, B, R, R, G, B, R, G, R, B.
In a frequency table of this data, what frequency is written for Red (R)?

Data: $21, 24, 24, 31, 33, 42$.
In a stem-and-leaf plot with the tens digit as the stem, which leaves are written beside the stem $2$?
foundation.solution_text:
The values in the $20$s are $21, 24, 24$
Leaves beside stem $2$: $1\;4\;4$

Read the height of the Veggie column
$=6\text{ slices}$

Read the height of the column centred on size $7$
$=8$

development.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.85]\draw[{Stealth}-{Stealth}] (-0.6,0)--(5.6,0);\foreach \x in {0,1,2,3,4,5} {\draw (\x,0)--(\x,-0.12); \node[below] at (\x,-0.14) {$\x$};}\fill (0,0.3) circle (2.2pt);\fill (0,0.62) circle (2.2pt);\fill (1,0.3) circle (2.2pt);\fill (1,0.62) circle (2.2pt);\fill (1,0.94) circle (2.2pt);\fill (1,1.26) circle (2.2pt);\fill (2,0.3) circle (2.2pt);\fill (2,0.62) circle (2.2pt);\fill (2,0.94) circle (2.2pt);\fill (3,0.3) circle (2.2pt);\fill (3,0.62) circle (2.2pt);\fill (5,0.3) circle (2.2pt);\node[below] at (2.5,-0.62) {Books borrowed in a week};\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The dot plot shows the number of books each student borrowed from the library in a week. How many students are shown in total?

Construct a stem-and-leaf plot for this data, using the tens digit as the stem:
$7, 12, 15, 15, 23, 24, 31$
development.solution_text:
1. **Choose the stems from the place value of the data.**
Stems are $0, 1, 2, 3$
2. **Write each leaf next to its stem.**
$\begin{array}{c|l}\text{Stem}&\text{Leaf}\\ \hline 0&7\\ 1&2\;5\;5\\ 2&3\;4\\ 3&1\end{array}$
Key: $1\;|\;2$ means $12$

development.question_text:
A frequency table lists these scores and frequencies: score $10 \to 4$, score $11 \to 7$, score $12 \to 7$, score $13 \to 2$.
In a frequency histogram of this table, which score(s) give the tallest column?
development.solution_text:
The tallest column matches the largest frequency, $7$
Scores $11$ and $12$ both have frequency $7$
Tallest columns: $11$ and $12$

mastery.question_text:
Construct a stem-and-leaf plot for this data, using the tens digit as the stem:
$19, 22, 22, 26, 28, 40, 53, 55, 56$
Which stem has no leaves, and why must it still be listed?
mastery.solution_text:
$\begin{array}{c|l}\text{Stem}&\text{Leaf}\\ \hline 1&9\\ 2&2\;2\;6\;8\\ 3&\\ 4&0\\ 5&3\;5\;6\end{array}$
Key: $1\;|\;9$ means $19$
Stem $3$ has no leaves.
It must still be listed because the stems run in an unbroken sequence, so the gap in the $30$s is visible.

mastery.question_text:
[tikz]
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.85]\draw[{Stealth}-{Stealth}] (-0.6,0)--(5.6,0);\foreach \x in {0,1,2,3,4,5} {\draw (\x,0)--(\x,-0.12); \node[below] at (\x,-0.14) {$\x$};}\fill (0,0.3) circle (2.2pt);\fill (0,0.62) circle (2.2pt);\fill (1,0.3) circle (2.2pt);\fill (1,0.62) circle (2.2pt);\fill (1,0.94) circle (2.2pt);\fill (1,1.26) circle (2.2pt);\fill (2,0.3) circle (2.2pt);\fill (2,0.62) circle (2.2pt);\fill (2,0.94) circle (2.2pt);\fill (3,0.3) circle (2.2pt);\fill (3,0.62) circle (2.2pt);\fill (5,0.3) circle (2.2pt);\node[below] at (2.5,-0.62) {Books borrowed in a week};\end{tikzpicture}
[/tikz]
The dot plot shows the number of books $12$ students borrowed in a week. What fraction of the students borrowed at least $3$ books?
mastery.solution_text:
At least $3$ books: $3$ books ($2$ students) and $5$ books ($1$ student)
$2+1=3\text{ students}$
$\frac{3}{12}$
$=\frac{1}{4}$
